1: The Philistines gather their forces to fight against Israel; Achish informs David that he and his men will join the campaign. David's response — 'you will know what your servant can do' — is deliberately ambiguous: it sounds like commitment but could be interpreted as a statement of capability without specifying for whom. David has spent over a year at Ziklag pretending to raid Judahite towns while actually raiding Amalekites and other southern peoples (1 Samuel 27:8-12). His loyalty to Achish is a performance, not a reality.
2: Achish's appointment of David as his permanent bodyguard is the highest expression of trust from a foreign king — the bodyguard role required absolute physical proximity and covenant loyalty. Achish genuinely believes David has defected from Saul and is now his loyal vassal. The political irony is complete: Israel's future king is about to march as a Philistine officer against the Israelite army containing his predecessor and his best friend.
3: The narrative resumes its retrospective: Samuel is dead, buried in Ramah, mourned by all Israel. The detail of Samuel's death (introduced in 1 Samuel 25:1) is now the crucial backstory for what follows. Saul had expelled the mediums and necromancers (bāʿălê hāʾôḇôṯ wəhayyiddəʿōnîm) earlier in his reign — this expulsion was covenant compliance with the Torah prohibition (Leviticus 19:31; 1 Samuel 20:27; Deuteronomy 18:11). Now, with Samuel dead and YHWH silent, Saul faces the ultimate oracular vacuum.
4: The Philistines encamp at Shunem in the Jezreel Valley; Saul gathers all Israel and encamps at Gilboa. The Jezreel Valley (modern Marj Ibn ʿAmir) is one of the great military corridors of the ancient Near East — it connects the coastal plain to the Jordan Valley, providing flat terrain ideal for chariot warfare. Shunem (modern Sulam) sits on the northern edge of the valley at the foot of the Hill of Moreh. Gilboa (Jebel Fuqūʿa) is the ridge on the south side, where Israel's defensive position would have been elevated but exposed to flanking.
5: Saul sees the Philistine camp and is afraid — his heart trembles greatly. The terror that seizes Saul reflects genuine strategic danger: the Philistine force was positioned to cut off Israelite territory and the Jezreel corridor. But the theological dimension is equally prominent: Saul's fear is the fear of a man who knows he has been abandoned by YHWH. He has no prophet (Samuel dead), no functioning oracle (Urim removed with the priestly line), and no dreams to rely on.
6: Saul inquires of YHWH through three channels: dreams, Urim, and prophets. YHWH does not answer through any of them. The three channels represent the three standard modes of divine communication in ancient Israel: nocturnal visions in sleep, the priestly binary oracle of the sacred lots (Urim and Thummim), and prophetic utterance. Saul's exclusion from all three simultaneously signals total divine abandonment. The Urim had already effectively passed to David (Abiathar took the ephod to David in 1 Samuel 22:20; 1 Samuel 23:6).
7: Saul instructs his servants to find a woman who is a bāʿalat-ʾôb — a mistress/keeper of a consulting spirit. The ʾôb was a chthonic spirit or ancestral shade that a specialist could invoke to communicate with the dead. Necromancy — consulting the spirits of the deceased for oracular guidance — was widespread in the ancient Near East. Mesopotamian texts describe elaborate rituals for summoning the dead (eṭemmu); Ugaritic texts reference necromantic specialists. The Endor woman is a professional in a technically illegal but apparently still-practiced trade.
8: Saul disguises himself and goes to Endor at night with two men. Endor (modern Khirbet Safsafeh) is on the north slope of the Hill of Moreh, between Shunem (the Philistine camp) and Gilboa (the Israelite camp) — Saul must pass around or through the Philistine positions to reach it, which explains the night travel. The disguise is pragmatic: the king who expelled all necromancers cannot appear in person at one's door.
9: The woman's fear when Saul asks her to consult a spirit reflects the genuine legal risk she faces. She has heard of Saul's expulsion decree and fears being caught by royal agents. Her caution — 'why are you laying a trap for my life to bring about my death?' — is the professional's recognition of entrapment tactics. Saul's reassurance ('as YHWH lives, you will have no trouble') is one of the most ironic oaths in the Hebrew Bible: the man who outlawed her practice swears by YHWH to protect her while asking her to do it.
10: Saul swears by YHWH that no punishment will come to the woman for this act. The oath by YHWH to protect someone while asking them to violate YHWH's law is the theological contradiction at the heart of the scene. Saul's willingness to invoke YHWH's name in the service of necromantic consultation epitomizes the disconnection between his religious language and his theological reality.
11: The woman asks whom she should bring up; Saul asks for Samuel. The verb heʿĕlāh (bring up) is the standard necromantic term — the dead are in Sheol, beneath; summoning them requires 'bringing up.' The woman's ability to consult Samuel specifically implies that she had a professional method for identifying and summoning particular shades rather than merely general ancestral spirits. Whether the appearance of Samuel represents an actual divine intrusion (YHWH overriding normal practice to confront Saul) or the expected result of her séance is a question the text leaves open.
12: When the woman sees Samuel, she shrieks and cries out to Saul: 'Why have you deceived me? You are Saul!' The woman's sudden recognition of Saul — coinciding with her vision of Samuel — suggests a connection in her mind between the appearance of the great prophet and the identity of the disguised client. The shriek may express genuine supernatural terror, or it may be the woman's realization that she has performed illegal magic for the king himself.
13: The woman describes seeing a divine being (ʾĕlōhîm) coming up from the earth. The term ʾĕlōhîm used for Samuel's shade is the same term used for God throughout the Hebrew Bible — in this context it describes the shade as a supernatural being with divine-realm status, not a mere ghost. Ancient Israelite theology located the spirits of the dead in a divine-realm liminal space (Sheol), giving them a quasi-divine categorization distinct from living humans.
14: Saul asks what the figure looks like; the woman says an old man wrapped in a robe (məʿîl). The robe is the identifying detail — it matches the garment whose corner David cut in 1 Samuel 24, and it is the distinctive garment associated with Samuel's priestly-prophetic role (cf. 1 Samuel 2:19; 1 Samuel 15:27). Saul bows to the ground — prostrating before the shade of the prophet he repeatedly disobeyed. The posture enacts the submission he refused in life.
15: Samuel's first words: 'Why have you disturbed me by bringing me up?' The disturbance of the dead was considered a serious transgression in ANE cultures — Mesopotamian texts warn against disturbing ancestral shades. Samuel's irritation is both realistic (within the ANE framework of the dead's rest) and pointed: Saul is trying to obtain from the dead prophet what he could not receive through legitimate means. Samuel's question — 'Why do you ask me, since YHWH has turned from you?' — identifies the real problem: the oracle vacuum is not technical but relational.
16: Samuel's declaration: 'YHWH has turned from you and become your enemy.' The Hebrew wayəhî rēʿeḵā — 'has become your adversary/enemy' — uses the same root as rāʿāh (evil). YHWH has moved from being Saul's patron to being his opponent. In ANE covenantal theology, the suzerain who was offended by the vassal could move from patron to punisher — the relationship did not end but reversed in valence. Saul's covenant violation has inverted YHWH's posture toward him.
17: Samuel reconfirms the transfer of the kingdom to David: 'YHWH has done to you as he spoke by me — he has torn the kingdom from your hand and given it to your neighbor David.' The word qāraʿ (torn) is the same word used when Saul's robe was torn in 1 Samuel 15:27-28, when Samuel said the kingdom would be torn from him. The tearing-robe acted as a prophetic sign; now Samuel announces its fulfillment.
18: Samuel identifies the specific reason: Saul did not obey YHWH's voice or execute his burning wrath against Amalek. The Amalek failure of 1 Samuel 15 — the incomplete ḥērem — is the named cause of the dynasty's fall. The connection between the Amalek disobedience and the Jezreel defeat is explicitly made by the shade of Samuel: the incomplete past judgment is now being completed in the present military crisis.
19: Samuel's final oracle: 'Tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me, and YHWH will give the army of Israel also into the hand of the Philistines.' The death sentence is specific in timing (tomorrow), in scope (Saul and his sons), and in its framing ('with me' in Sheol). The oracle delivers what Saul came for — a word about the battle's outcome — but it is the worst possible word. The séance has succeeded technically and failed completely for Saul.
20: Saul falls full length on the ground, overwhelmed by fear and by the fact that he has eaten nothing all day and night. The physical collapse enacts his psychological state: there is nothing left. The detail of fasting heightens his physical depletion — already weakened by hunger, the terrifying oracle leaves him prostrate. The woman and Saul's servants must encourage him to eat before he can move.
21: The woman comes to Saul and sees he is greatly troubled. She urges him to eat, citing that she took her life in her hands to obey him. The domestic scene of a woman preparing bread for the king in the middle of the night — following a séance in which his death was pronounced — is among the most poignant in Samuel. The woman who practiced the forbidden art becomes an unlikely nurse to the dying king.
22: Saul refuses at first — he has been lying prostrate and will not rise to eat. His refusal may be the paralysis of grief or a last remnant of cultic awareness: eating before a military action could have ritual dimensions. But the servants join the woman in urging him to eat and regain strength for the journey.
23: The servants join the woman in urging Saul to eat. He listens, rises from the ground, and sits on the bed. The act of rising and sitting is the minimal restoration of functional posture — he is not renewed, not healed, not hopeful; merely upright enough to continue.
24: The woman slaughters a fattened calf that she has in the house — a significant domestic animal reserved for special occasions — and bakes unleavened bread. The meal she prepares is a final royal feast: fatted calf and fresh bread in the middle of the night for a king who will die the next day. The hospitality is generous and darkly ironic — she honors the man who outlawed her.
25: The food is set before Saul and his servants; they eat, rise, and leave that same night. The chapter ends in darkness: Saul and his men slip back through the night to their forces at Gilboa, carrying only the knowledge of their imminent deaths. The journey back — through or around the Philistine camp at Shunem — is the condemned man's walk to the place of execution.
